Infant Population R Assignment

Alyssah Mack

Simulate a population of 10,000 infants whose mean weight is 3300g and the standard deviation is 500g.

infant_birth_weight <- rnorm(10000, mean=3300, sd=500)

Take 10 random samples from the infant population

random10 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=10)

Find and record the mean of the sample

mean10 <- mean(random10)

Repeat this 10,000 times

Result10 <- numeric(length=10000)

for(i in 1:10000){
  random10 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=10)
  mean10 <- mean(random10)
  #store the mean in Result10 vector
  Result10[i] <- mean10
  }

Plot all of the sample means on a histogram

hist(Result10, main="Distribution of Sample Means n=10", 
     xlab="Sample means", 
     col="cornflowerblue", 
     xlim=c(2600,3900))

Repeat the same steps for:

n = 100

random100 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=100)
mean100 <- mean(random100)
Result100 <- numeric(length=10000)

for(i in 1:10000){
  random100 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=100)
  mean100 <- mean(random100)
  Result100[i] <- mean100}

hist(Result100, main="Distribution of Sample Means n=100", 
     xlab="Sample means", 
     col="lightpink")

n = 1,000

random1000 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=1000)
mean1000 <- mean(random1000)
Result1000 <- numeric(length=10000)

for(i in 1:10000){
  random1000 <- sample(infant_birth_weight, size=1000)
  mean1000 <- mean(random1000)
  Result1000[i] <- mean1000}

hist(Result1000, main= "Distribution of Sample Means n=1000", 
     xlab="Sample means", 
     col="mediumpurple1")

Interpretation

Generally, the random samples taken from the initial normal distribution of infant weights also follow a normal distribution in that each distribution of sample means has a central mean with thinning tails. As the sample size increases, the distribution behaves more like a normal distribution, reinforcing the idea that larger sample sizes are more reflective of populations.
